primary_diagnosis.name: Acute cardiogenic pulmonary oedema (hypertensive acute left-ventricular failure) with impending respiratory failure

pathophysiology_summary: Acute decompensation of chronic left-ventricular failure, precipitated by a hypertensive surge, a missed diuretic dose and an unworn CPAP mask overnight. A sharp rise in afterload plus fluid retention raises left-atrial and pulmonary-capillary hydrostatic pressure until fluid floods the alveoli, producing the classic frothy, pink (blood-tinged) sputum, profound orthopnoea and paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnoea. Alveolar flooding impairs gas exchange, causing severe hypoxia, accessory-muscle use and diaphoresis from sympathetic drive; untreated it progresses to hypoxaemic respiratory arrest. The correct emergency physiology is to offload the ventricle (vasodilatation with nitrates to drop preload and afterload, diuresis to remove volume) and support oxygenation with CPAP/BiPAP — interventions delivered by paramedics and the ED, not achievable in a telehealth chat.

differential.diagnosis: acute cardiogenic pulmonary oedema / acute left-ventricular failure
differential.evidence_basis: Sudden nocturnal breathlessness relieved only by sitting bolt upright (orthopnoea/PND) with frothy pink sputum in a patient on a diuretic and antihypertensive.
Acute breathlessness and cough — but does not explain frothy pink sputum, orthopnoea/PND or leg swelling; distinguished on exam by wheeze rather than fine bibasal crackles and no S3.
Sudden dyspnoea — but the fluid-overload picture (leg swelling, frothy sputum) points to pump failure rather than a primary vascular occlusion.
Cough and breathlessness — but abrupt onset waking from sleep, no fever, and bilateral basal crackles argue against infection.
Chest tightness, diaphoresis and impending doom in acute pulmonary oedema — ACS is a common precipitant of flash pulmonary oedema and must be actively excluded with ECG/troponin.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening, cardiogenic pulmonary oedema should already lead on orthopnoea + frothy pink sputum; asthma/COPD is the dangerous anchoring alternative.

differential.diagnosis: acute cardiogenic pulmonary oedema / acute left-ventricular failure
differential.evidence_basis: Diagnostic tetrad complete: profound orthopnoea, frothy pink sputum, cold clammy diaphoresis and leg swelling — plus missed diuretic and unworn CPAP as precipitants.
Frothy pink sputum, orthopnoea/PND and peripheral oedema are not features of primary airways disease; treating as asthma with a bronchodilator is the key error of commission.
Still cannot be excluded without ECG/troponin — must be worked up in the ED as a possible precipitant of the pulmonary oedema.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After the red-flag screen the diagnosis is clinically clear; the residual must-not-miss is the ACS precipitant, which is an ED workup rather than a telehealth determination.

differential.diagnosis: acute cardiogenic pulmonary oedema (hypertensive acute left-ventricular failure)
differential.evidence_basis: Confirmed clinical picture with impending respiratory failure; time-critical, requires immediate ambulance for nitrates, diuresis and CPAP/BiPAP.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final: a single time-critical emergency diagnosis; the AI Doctor is scored on recognition + T5 escalation, not on remote confirmation.

red_flags.description: Profound orthopnoea / paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnoea — cannot breathe unless sitting bolt upright, woken from sleep
red_flags.clinical_significance: Hallmark of acute left-ventricular failure with alveolar flooding; severity here signals impending respiratory failure.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Progression to hypoxaemic respiratory arrest within a short time if not offloaded and oxygenated.

red_flags.clinical_significance: Indicates fluid (with red cells) flooding the alveoli — a hallmark of acute pulmonary oedema and a marker of severity.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Misattribution to a chest infection or asthma delays the life-saving offloading and oxygen support.

red_flags.description: Severe hypoxia with accessory-muscle use and exhaustion from the work of breathing (clinician SpO2 88 percent on room air)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Impending respiratory failure — the patient is tiring; a falling conscious level heralds arrest.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Respiratory fatigue leads to hypercapnia, obtundation and arrest.

red_flags.description: Cold, clammy, diaphoretic, pale skin — poor peripheral perfusion in a hypertensive crisis (clinician BP 185/105, HR 115)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Sympathetic surge and poor perfusion in hypertensive acute heart failure; marks a high-acuity crisis needing emergency vasodilatation and diuresis.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Anchoring on the breathlessness and cough and reaching for a salbutamol/reliever inhaler or nebuliser — this treats a wheeze the patient does not have and delays the definitive offloading and oxygen support.
Telling a breathless patient to lie down or 'rest flat' — in pulmonary oedema this floods more alveoli and can precipitate arrest; the patient must stay bolt upright.
Giving oral or IV fluids to someone unwell and breathless — additional volume worsens pulmonary oedema.

Deferring escalation because no SpO2/BP can be measured remotely — the clinical picture (frothy sputum, orthopnoea, exhaustion) is sufficient to call 000 now; the AI must not under-act pending a device reading.
Treating the pulmonary oedema and forgetting that acute coronary syndrome is a frequent precipitant that itself needs ECG/troponin and ED workup.

risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: Severe / high-acuity — SpO2 88 percent, RR 32, hypertensive (185/105), diaphoretic, exhausted
risk_scores.clinical_implication: Immediate emergency (000) transport for CPAP/BiPAP, nitrates and diuresis; monitor for respiratory arrest.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Auscultation of bilateral fine inspiratory crackles from the bases to the mid-zones
Graded bilateral pitting oedema (2+ to mid-calf)
Measured vital signs — BP 185/105, HR 115, RR 32, SpO2 88 percent, temperature 36.6
Chest radiograph (cardiomegaly, prominent upper-lobe vessels, Kerley B lines)
12-lead ECG and troponin to exclude an ACS precipitant
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise acute cardiogenic pulmonary oedema from the history and carer-observable signs and call 000 immediately for an emergency ambulance; keep the patient bolt upright (never lie flat), coach slow breathing, and hand over to paramedics for oxygen/CPAP, nitrates and IV diuresis.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low for the syndrome (the constellation is classic); the real risk is UNDER-action — waiting for a measured SpO2/BP, mislabelling as asthma and giving a bronchodilator, or advising the patient to lie down.

guidelines_referenced.reference: NHFA/CSANZ Australian clinical guidelines for the management of heart failure 2018
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Acute pulmonary oedema is a medical emergency: sit the patient up, give oxygen/non-invasive ventilation, IV nitrates for vasodilatation and IV loop diuretic; identify and treat precipitants (including ACS).
